data.title: Meta Platforms: The Recent Breakout From $600 To $700 Is Just The Beginning
data.description:
It's been a great year for Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META) shareholders, as shares extended their previous rally by an additional 49.18%. The stock price has come a long way, and investors who went against the grain in November of 2022 when META's stock price dipped below $100 have been rewarded handsomely as shares of META have climbed over 675% as they have increased from around $90 to over $700. I am still very bullish on META, and while some investors may be taking profits, I believe it's one of the more undervalued stocks in the Magnificent Seven. One of the big investing themes that, I think, will play out over the next several years is that big tech will just get bigger, and I am not looking to play that catch-up trade in small caps. META has more than 1/3rd of the global population using its products on a daily basis, and when businesses look to advertise, META's Platforms offer one of the best paths to their target audience. META has a lot of opportunities on the horizon that should drive revenue growth and EPS, including wearables, platforms, and generative AI. The share price could decline a bit from here, but META's focus on utilizing generative AI to enhance its product offerings should create a lot of value for shareholders going forward. I am still very bullish on META, and I think their best days are ahead of them.

Following up on my previous article about Meta Platforms

The last time I wrote an article about META was in the middle of August (can be read here). Since then, shares of META have appreciated by 33.82% compared to the S&P 500, which has climbed 13.29%. When the dividend is taken into account, shares of META have increased by 24.06% over this period. I had previously discussed how META was driving increased levels of revenue and profitability from its operations and the impacts of its advancements in AI from Q2 earnings. A lot has changed since then. As the Fed started cutting rates, a new administration took office, and META continued to increase its revenue and profitability. I am following up with a new article to discuss why I am still very bullish on META after shares have increased by almost 50% in the past year. I think there is still a significant opportunity on the horizon for META as they are on the forefront of the AI revolution, and the surface has only begun to be scratched.

Risks to investing in Meta Platforms

While I am bullish on META, and it's one of the largest companies in the market, there are still significant risk factors that investors should be aware of. META has been at the center of negative publicity in the past, which has impacted its share price. If they receive unfavorable media coverage around content handling, it could reduce confidence in META's brands, which could impact its business. META has a treasure trove of information on individuals and businesses, which makes it a target for cyberattacks. If META incurs a cyber breach, it could compromise user data, impact META's reputation, and lead to a loss of revenue. META is one of the largest advertising businesses, and its competitors are continuously trying to figure out ways to penetrate its moat. Amazon (AMZN) and Netflix (NFLX) have both tried to figure out ways to boost their advertising dollars as they have valuable space on their platforms and large amounts of traffic using their app and websites each day. If competitors are able to provide a better ROI than META on advertising dollars, then it would be difficult for META to retain users. There is also a risk from generative AI because META is allocating tens of billions toward R&D, and its efforts could get upstaged by competitors. Investors should do their own due diligence as there are many risk factors regarding allocating capital toward META.

Why I believe Meta Platforms is still undervalued after the recent breakout

META finished 2024 in a position of strength, and after going through their financials, there is a lot to be excited about. META finished the year with $77.82 billion in cash on hand between their marketable securities and cash equivalents. They also have an additional 6.07 billion in non-marketable equity securities, with only $28.83 billion in long-term debt. META operates a cash-rich business, and its balance sheet is one of the strongest in the market. META's financials put them in a position where they can ride out difficult macroeconomic events, and the fluctuations in interest rates don't necessarily impact them directly. META has built a company that is generating $62.36 billion of net income and has a return on equity (ROE) of 34.14%. META continues to generate excessive amounts of cash from its operations, which is allowing them to organically grow and drive value for shareholders.

META has been able to drive larger amounts of revenue from its platforms by increasing its ability to drive value from its ads. META's ad impressions increased by 11% YoY, with substantial growth from Asia-Pacific and Rest of World regions. While increasing its ad impressions, META also increased the price per ad by 10% in 2024. META's improvements to ad performance through enhanced targeting and measurement tools created a higher demand for its ads, and companies were willing to absorb the increases. META has also leveraged AI to improve its ad delivery, targeting, and measurement capabilities. This had a drastic impact on the financials in 2024 as META's revenue increased by $29.6 billion (21.94%) YoY to $164.5 billion. META was already a company that was generating more than $100 billion in revenue, and the demand for their services was so high that they witnessed an increase of more than 20% YoY. META was also able to drive higher margins from its business as it had a 37.91% profit margin, which increased from 28.98% YoY. META's profitability grew by 59.5% YoY as it generated $62.36 billion in net income. META's ability to increase revenue and profitability is a testament to its business's staying power and how its platforms provide value to its users.

Some investors and media outlets have questioned META's allocation toward CapEx, yet I am not concerned. I believe anyone who is worried about a $65 billion allocation toward CapEx in 2025 isn't looking at the bigger picture. On the Q4 conference call, Susan Li (META CFO) discussed the $65 billion outlay for CapEx that META was budgeting for in 2025. The YoY increase of roughly $25 billion is to support META's generative AI efforts and its core businesses. Over the past decade, META has directed $172.1 billion toward CapEx, and while there have been questions regarding the large spend, the results have been accreditive for shareholders. Spending $172.1 billion on CapEx has allowed META to generate $425.68 billion in cash from operations over the same period. META has produced $253.47 billion in free cash flow (FCF), which has allowed it to repurchase $189.99 billion worth of shares since 2015 and pay $5.07 billion in dividends in 2024. META has established a track record of growing its cash from operations and FCF as the investments into CapEx increase. I believe that the investments META is making will make its products more alluring to its customers and drive additional profitability from its operations.

When I look at how the market is valuing META compared to Magnificent Seven and companies such as Walmart Inc. (WMT) and Costco Wholesale (COST), I think there is still value that needs to be unlocked. META is trading at 27.55 times 2025 earnings and is expected to grow its earnings by 33.28% over the next 2 years, which places its 2027 P/E at 20.67. META is trading at the 2nd lowest valuation in the Magnificent Seven on both 2025 and 2027 earnings. The fact that WMT is being valued at 31.38 times 2027 earnings when they are expected to generate 24.5% of EPS growth over the next 2 years is an indication that META trading at 20.67 times 2027 earnings is an opportunity when META is projected to grow its EPS by 33.28% over the same period. When I look at the way META allocates capital and how they are able to grow profitability, there is no question in my mind that, over the long term, META's shares will continue to appreciate.

There are several opportunities that I am excited about that, I believe, will drive revenue higher for Meta Platforms

The massive amount of CapEx being allocated is allowing META to make significant advances across its family of apps. Through Meta AI, the user experience will improve through leveraging user data to focus on personalization. Meta AI will utilize specific details shared by users in one-on-one chats to personalize its responses and improve the algorithm. Meta AI will pull from all of its apps to improve content recommendations as it will have a more comprehensive understanding of the user's interests and preferences. If Meta AI can improve META's platforms and create longer engagement, it could charge more for advertising, which would likely increase the top and bottom line.

The opportunities in AI are extensive for META, as they are innovating in AI technologies to build new experiences across its products. It looks like META will focus on generative AI to make its platforms more immersive as they are creating tools to improve ad delivery, targeting, and measurement capabilities. META believes that its personal AI assistant will reach over 1 billion people on the commercial side, while internally, META believes it will be possible to build AI engineering agents that can code at the same level as mid-level engineers. It's been controversial, but moving beyond the traditional 2D screen has been debated for quite some time. I think that the inroads that META has created will allow it to be the frontrunner in a new computing frontier where users will experience virtual, mixed, and augmented reality through wearable devices. This is META's chance to own the hardware and be the tollbooth that app creators pay a fee to, unlike how META's products are currently situated with Alphabet (GOOGL)(GOOG) and Apple (AAPL). In 2025, META expects to spend approximately 50% of Reality Labs operating expenses on wearables initiatives and 50% on metaverse initiatives. This could pay off in spades if widespread adoption occurs, as META will be a winner that takes most in the next generation of computing.

Conclusion

While META has experienced a multi-year bull run since November of 2022, I don't think the party is over. I believe that shares of META are undervalued as they are trading at less than 28 times 2025 earnings and 21 times 2027 earnings. The analyst community expects 33.28% EPS growth from the end of 2025 through 2027, and there could be more growth on the horizon as the wearable division expands. All of the CapEx that has been allocated to META's products has allowed them to generate larger levels of profitability and direct more capital toward share repurchases and dividends. META has a long history of increasing profitability, and when I look at their previous endeavors, the $65 billion being spent on CapEx in 2025 could have strong ramifications on their future earnings potential. I am still very bullish on META and think that shares will see $1,000 over the next 2 years.

data.title: Allspring Special Large Cap Value Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

Equity markets and large-cap value stocks experienced significant volatility during the quarter. The first two months of the quarter saw the Russell 1000 Value Index move higher, as confidence around a “soft landing” increased following the Federal Reserve (Fed) rate cuts and optimism around a Trump election win. This optimism began to diminish in late November as investors began to discount a potentially slower and shallower rate cutting cycle and a December rally in the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield (US10Y). The Russell 1000 Value Index returned -1.98% for the quarter. Within the index, the financials (+7%) and communication services (+2%) sectors were the best performing in the quarter. Meanwhile, the materials (-12%) and health care (-10%) sectors were the worst-performing sectors within the index.

The fund underperformed its benchmark for the quarter, as the early-quarter rally was led by the higher beta and more cyclical portions of the index that investors believed would benefit from the transition in both monetary and fiscal policy. However, the portfolio outperformed the index in the December pull back, as we expect our process to do, as well as for the full-year period.

Portfolio Positioning: Quarterly Attribution and Analysis

Stock selection in communication services and materials contributed. Within communication services, Alphabet Inc. (GOOG,GOOGL) , the parent company of Google, provides the dominant search advertising platform, Android smartphone software, YouTube, and the Google Cloud platform. Some positive returns on Alphabet's significant investments into artificial intelligence have shown through with accelerating growth in search and cloud revenue, leading to the stock’s strong performance. We remain confident in management’s consistent investments in its innovation and technology advancements that provide multiple avenues of revenue growth, which, combined with a pristine balance sheet and ongoing cost optimization, result in an attractive reward/risk valuation. Stock selection in consumer staples and industrials detracted. In the industrials sector, Canadian Pacific Kansas City Ltd. (CP, CPKC) operates a transnational rail network. Through the acquisition of Kansas City Southern Railroad, CPKC’s network now offers the only single-line haul service connecting Canada to the U.S. and down through Mexico. We expect its lines to be among the lowest-cost and energy-efficient shipping options for bulk cargo in North America, and this advantage should allow the consolidation of market share to CPKC over time. CPKC underperformed the market as fears around Trump's trade policy and its potential impact on cross-border rail volumes garnered attention. We believe the sentiment swoon to be an overreaction.

Portfolio Positioning: Trailing 12-Month Attribution Analysis

The fund outpaced its benchmark over the 12-month period, driven primarily by stock selection. The Russell 1000 Value Index returned 14.37%, despite significant volatility throughout the year. Stock selection in real estate, consumer discretionary, and materials added value. A large contributor was General Motors Co. (GM), an American auto manufacturer. GM has a leading market-share position in large pickup trucks that carries pricing power and generates strong free cash flow to help facilitate balance sheet deployment to pursue organic growth opportunities. GM continued to exceed expectations as it focuses on profitability. GM also committed to prudent spending on capital. Instead of additional investments, GM believes a better use of its balance sheet is to return capital to shareholders.

Stock selection in energy and health care were the largest detractors. Within health care, Humana Inc. (HUM) is a large managed-care organization. Management recently reported an unexpected loss, lowered guidance for fiscal-year 2024, and guided to a weaker range for 2025 earnings-per-share growth due to higher-than-expected costs in the Medicare Advantage sector. Also, regulatory trends and political rhetoric in the industry have been a headwind; however, Humana has maintained a strong underlying cash flow profile, and we believe it should be able to price insurance plans for 2025 and start to recover this margin compression.

Portfolio Positioning: Outlook

The fund made minor changes to positioning during the three-month period. The fund increased its weight in industrials and information technology and decreased it in real estate and materials as reward/risk ratios dictated. We expect continued market volatility in 2025 given the increased uncertainty around future fiscal and monetary policy. The new administration has brought some optimism around deregulation and an extension of previous corporate tax cuts; however, there is still much uncertainty around the impact of increased tariffs and rising geopolitical tensions. The path of future interest rate cuts could be slower and shallower than investor expectations as the Fed digests evolving labor and inflation data points. This macroeconomic uncertainty only strengthens our view that portfolios should be balanced with sound risk controls. Our portfolio construction process is designed to isolate stock selection as the primary source of projected tracking error and relative returns. We believe that companies will need to increasingly rely on their balance sheets to navigate the macro uncertainty. Pricing power will also be essential to protect margins and sustain cash flows due to rising tariff pressures, labor challenges, and supply chain modifications.

data.title: Renewable And Regulatory Uncertainty Suggests Continuing Trouble For CVR Energy
data.description:
Just a little over a year ago I suggested that CVR Energy (NYSE:CVI) might be worth buying for strong total returns due to improving refining crack spreads (at the time), and biodiesel fuel investments, along with improving fertilizer prices in early 2024. In addition, I had noted that famed investor Carl Icahn, who is part-owner of the company along with Icahn Enterprise (IEP) and CVR Partners (UAN), had a large ownership stake in CVI. This was what I wrote back in January 2024:

Icahn holds about 66% of the outstanding shares (units) of CVR Energy, even after selling about 4M units back in September. CVI is mostly a refiner and compared to its peers it has performed much better over the past 10 years.

Then in November 2024, Icahn boosted his stake in CVI up to 80% by cutting the IEP distribution in half.

At the time I had also noted that CVR Energy owned 37% of the units of CVR Partners and that rising fertilizer prices in the first half of 2023 had started to decline later in the year and were negatively impacting the UAN stock price, which in turn caused CVI stock to decline along with it.

CVR Energy also owns the general partner and 37% of the units of CVR Partners, so the performance of UAN stock and the nitrogen fertilizer business also directly impacts CVI. Fertilizer prices were recovering in 2023 up until about the end of October but have been sliding since then as shown on this price chart from YCharts.

However, in the year since I wrote that article with a Buy recommendation for CVI, the price and total return for UAN far outperformed CVI. In fact, UAN delivered a reasonable 1-year return (as of 2/20/25) of about 23% compared to a loss of -38% from CVI.

A big reason for that significant decline in CVI stock in 2024 was due to the failed $8B bid for Citgo followed by a suspension of the dividend. I downgraded my CVI rating to a Sell in October 2024 based on that news and other macro factors that spelled trouble for CVR Energy heading into 2025.

CVR Energy Q4 2024 Earnings Summary

This week CVR Energy reported Q4 2024 earnings on February 18 and the market responded positively. Even though revenues declined by -11% and earnings were negative, the report beat estimates on both the top and bottom line. Perhaps investors are now hoping that the bottom is in, and the current price offers a buying opportunity.

Here is a summary of the Q4 report from the press release:

CVR Energy, Inc. today announced fourth quarter 2024 net income attributable to CVR Energy stockholders of $28 million, or 28 cents per diluted share, compared to fourth quarter 2023 net income attributable to CVR Energy stockholders of $91 million, or 91 cents per diluted share. Adjusted loss for the fourth quarter of 2024 was 13 cents per diluted share compared to adjusted earnings of 65 cents per diluted share in the fourth quarter of 2023. Net income for the fourth quarter of 2024 was $40 million, compared to net income of $97 million in the fourth quarter of 2023. Fourth quarter 2024 EBITDA was $122 million, compared to fourth quarter 2023 EBITDA of $204 million. Adjusted EBITDA for the fourth quarter of 2024 was $67 million, compared to adjusted EBITDA of $170 million in the fourth quarter of 2023.

In addition, the company enhanced liquidity by $408m in Q4 with a new term loan and the sale of a 50 percent interest in Midway Pipeline. The company generated free cash flow of $40 million in Q4. Total liquidity stood at $1.1 billion as of the end of 2024.

Admittedly, refining market conditions remained difficult in Q4 as explained by CEO Dave Lamp on the earnings call, however, the outlook for 2025 was cautiously optimistic:

Refining market conditions remained challenging in the fourth quarter, largely due to the market being oversupplied as a result of both average utilization levels in the United States as well as addition of new refining capacity globally. As we look to 2025, however, we are cautiously optimistic that refining market conditions will improve relative to 2024 for a number of reasons.

The CVI stock price rose sharply after the report and remains above $20 but is at high risk of performing poorly as indicated by SA on the CVI Summary page.

Despite the apparent turnaround in stock price performance, I maintain my Sell rating on CVI given the poor macroeconomic outlook for refiners and the even more dire outlook for renewables in 2025 as a result of the new Trump administration policies that will make ongoing investments in renewables even less appealing going forward. In fact, during his remarks on the Q4 earnings call CEO Lamp discussed the headwinds that the company is facing in the renewables business.

Given these headwinds, as the renewable space moves forward, it is difficult to ignore that we have invested approximately $290 million in our renewable business over the last several years to participate in carbon emission reduction to generate RINs and optimize our assets we have. In doing so, we have been reminded that reliance on government credits is not a sustainable business, and we already have enough exposure to politically mismanaged regulations like RFS.

As a result, we are left with an investment with uncertain returns in the business that today is breakeven at best. We have completed the design of SAF RD project near our Coffeyville facilities, have a firm understanding of our capability to convert our Wynnewood renewable diesel unit to SAF production with additional capital.

Potential for these opportunities in the future, it is critical to get clarity on availability and durability of government subsidies before we continue investing additional capital or time into such ventures. We remain willing to participate further in this space but are pausing our intentions to actively pursue the market for partners and investors.

Some additional information from the AI-generated earnings call insights suggested that renewable capacity was decreased in Q4 and that negative catalysts remain putting constraints on renewable feedstock optimization.

  • Renewable diesel production capacity has been reduced to 80 million gallons annually due to catalyst limitations. Management noted a catalyst-related degradation impacted December performance.
  • Matthew Blair, Tudor Pickering: Sought clarity on capture rates and renewable diesel feedstock mix. CFO Neumann noted slight tailwinds to Q4 capture rates due to inventory benefits, and CEO Lamp highlighted catalyst constraints limiting feedstock optimization.

Analyst sentiment was subdued although cautiously optimistic of a market recovery in the refining segment. The SA Quant ratings suggest a Strong Sell with very poor factor grades in nearly every category except Valuation.

Refining Peers are Struggling, Too

CVR Energy is not the only US-based refiner that delivered poor performance over the past year. Several of the company’s peers like Calumet (CLMT), PBF Energy (PBF), and Delek US Holdings (DK) have also reported negative total returns in the last 12 months and are not overly optimistic regarding the 2025 outlook.

In fact, PBF plunged to its lowest level in three years after reporting a larger than expected loss in Q4. Calumet was cut to a Sell rating by UBS due to regulatory uncertainty.

The uncertainty in the renewables segment is especially an issue to watch with CVI and the CEO was somewhat defensive to the point of being almost antagonistic when asked about the outlook for potential opportunities in that segment during the earnings call Q&A:

Q: Adam Wijaya

Fair. And just a follow-up for me on the Renewables segment. I saw that you guys broke the segment earnings profile out this quarter. And when we think about the path to run rate, positive EBITDA contribution going forward, aside from improving margins more broadly. Operationally, is there anything you think we should be focused on?

And then can you tie that into how you guys think about potential opportunities within renewables specifically in SAF kind of what's needed from a supply-demand standpoint or an incentive standpoint to further make a call there?

A: Dave Lamp

Sure, Adam. I mean, as I mentioned in the prepared remarks, is the problem with renewables is the uncertainty of government subsidies. And there they will swing wildly. I guess, I'd say it. And when I say that it's politically driven RFS regulation that seems to be there's nobody driving the ship. And I'll just use the example of the fact that the mandate is 22 billion and 15, 16 of that is ethanol. Ethanol does little for the environment, whereas renewable diesel does.

And lo and behold, you've got D6s coupled with D4s, that's starting to break a little bit. But even so, you would think the government would drive towards lower carbon material rather than just doing a mandate on ethanol, which is really not necessary at all because ethanol is a part of the fuel blend. It's a cheap blend stock for octane, and it would be blended no matter what.

So until we get clarity on some of these regulations and somebody in adults in the room controlling them. It'd be difficult to see how we make some investments. Just to give you an idea on SAF, the subsidies -- today, the people are reporting that that's selling for about $1 to $2 premium over R&D. If you take all the subsidies and add them all up, you need more like $4 to really -- to even have that kind of sales price.

So to us, it's -- we've got -- we've had all we can stand of exposure to government subsidies and it's going to take a shift change for us to really invest in -- we do have projects that look attractive on a capital per barrel capital basis for both SAF and renewables. But again, these subsidies are just scary.

Summary: Avoid CVI Stock

With the new Trump 2.0 administration’s energy policies and plans to roll back some of the IRA legislation passed during the Biden administration, the landscape for renewable energy regulation is changing. This has created consternation and frustration on the part of companies like CVR Energy that have made substantial investments in renewable energy infrastructure over the past several years. Until and unless they can find a buyer for the renewable segment, I believe that CVI stock will continue to underperform and I maintain my Sell rating.

data.title: The London Company Income Equity Vs. Russell 1000 Value Full Year 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Full Year Market Update

U.S. equities posted another strong year of performance in 2024, in almost a carbon copy of 2023's robust gains. An easing of macro risks (lower inflation and a weakening labor market), a shift at the Federal Reserve to less restrictive monetary policy, and a late-year election bump drove market gains. And like last year, there was a wide dichotomy in leadership as the market favored larger cap growth over smaller cap value. While equity markets delivered strong headline gains in 2024, these returns were largely driven by multiple expansion, leaving valuations elevated and the market increasingly reliant on a handful of mega-cap leaders. Looking at market factors, Growth, Volatility, Size, and Momentum factors had the most positive impact on relative returns, while Value, Yield, and most of the Quality factors presented headwinds.

Key Performance Takeaways for the Year

  • The London Company Income Equity portfolio returned 12.3% (11.9% net) year-to-date vs. a 14.4% increase in the Russell 1000 Value Index. Stock selection was a headwind to relative performance, partially offset by positive sector exposure.

  • For the year, the Income Equity portfolio slightly trailed the Russell 1000 Value index, but strong relative performance in the second half brought up our upside participation to 85-90%-in line with expectations. The portfolio made up ground in 3Q as factors shifted our way. However, headwinds from exposure to Quality and Yield factors detracted from relative performance for the year.

  • Our portfolio positioning is always consistent with a focus on risk reduction, and we believe this bodes well for our approach in an expensive, concentrated market. We believe our focus on quality, diversification, and shareholder will continue to reinforce our margin of safety, positioning Income Equity portfolio for success in this uncertain climate.

Top 3 Contributors to Relative Performance

  • Progressive Corporation (PGR) - PGR outperformed during the year due to its success in growing the business and improving margins. PGR has gained market share from competitors and improved its margins by effectively segmenting underwriting risks and implementing strategic pricing. PGR has achieved its profitability target by lowering advertising costs and focusing on acquiring preferred customers. We remain confident in PGR's ability to execute in all environments, competitive advantages, and capital allocation strategy.

  • Apple Inc. (AAPL) - Shares of AAPL rallied throughout the year after posting strong results with improvement in China and positive AI enhancements to products. AAPL also announced a $110B share repurchase authorization and a 4% increase in the dividend. iPhone revenue was down a bit although there were some moving pieces that made year-over-year comparisons challenging. Its large installed based and services are an important long-term driver of the business.

  • Philip Morris International Inc. (PM) - Shares of PM outperformed during the year mainly due to strong execution and an improving outlook. The smoke-free category remains the key driver with notable growth from ZYN nicotine pouches. IQOS growth is being fueled by innovation and smoker conversions, while the U.S. presents a major untapped commercialization opportunity. The successful portfolio shift has resulted in smoke-free products now accounting for nearly 40% of total net revenue and gross profit. Guidance points to continued acceleration in reduced-risk product adoption, which should drive overall volume growth and market share gains. We believe the combination of smoke-free growth potential and a resilient combustibles portfolio will generate significant and sustainable free cash flow in the years ahead.

Top 3 Detractors from Relative Performance

  • Nestle S.A. (OTCPK:NSRGY) - NSRGY shares underperformed the broader market all year. Sentiment across the packaged food space is weak as it emerges from two years of unprecedented food price inflation. NSRGY's latest earnings report did not suggest an acceleration in organic growth from current subdued trends. NSRGY's portfolio is attractively positioned in categories that have stable, long-term volume tailwinds such as coffee, pet food, and nutritional health. Barring further executional missteps, we believe the downside to the current stock price is low.

  • Merck & Co., Inc. (MRK) - MRK's underperformance for the year reflected a weaker outlook for its HPV vaccine amid challenges in the China market, along with concerns about the incoming administration. Certain pharma stocks have come under pressure since the election and subsequent Presidential nominations for healthcare positions, as the market digests potential policy implications. Despite these headwinds, MRK continues to show strength across its portfolio, particularly with Keytruda, which is driving growth and showing promise in new launches and combination therapies. The company has successfully expanded its pipeline through effective R&D and acquisitions. We believe MRK's strong drug and vaccine portfolio combined with effective leadership positions it well for continued growth.

  • United Parcel Service, Inc. (UPS) - UPS underperformed this year due to the challenging transportation and parcel market. Many foreign economies have been weak in recent months and economic growth in the U.S. has shifted to services from goods (negative for shipping companies). Weaker revenue growth along with higher union contract wages led to lower margins. While recent results have been lackluster, we maintain our confidence in the long-term growth opportunities in the global package delivery business. The dividend yield is roughly 5% and management continues to return cash to shareholders via dividends and share repurchase at attractive prices.

Sector Influence

We are bottom-up stock pickers, but sector exposures influenced relative performance as follows:

  • What Helped: Underweight Health Care & Energy (two weaker performing sectors)

  • What Hurt: Underweight Utilities (a better performing sector) & overweight Information Technology (a weaker performing sector)

Portfolio Characteristics & Positioning

We believe the Income Equity portfolio is positioned for long-term durability and possesses the fundamental ingredients that stand the test of time: wide moats, sustainably high returns on capital, strong free cash flow generation, low leverage ratios, and an attractive shareholder yield (dividends + net buybacks). The portfolio trades at a premium to the Value index, but we believe this is justified as companies in the Value indices have lower returns on capital and higher leverage. As a corporate debt maturity wall approaches and the cost of that capital stays elevated, we believe companies with strong balance sheets and the ability to self-fund their operations could have a structural advantage in 2025 and beyond. In an environment of possibly lower returns and greater volatility, we believe the Income Equity portfolio offers an attractive option for equity investors.

Looking Ahead

As we enter 2025, we believe the market faces an inflection point where sustaining momentum becomes increasingly difficult. Across the real economy, demand still seems sluggish and clear late-cycle signals persist. Revenue growth and corporate profits have leaned on inflationary pricing, but margins face growing headwinds as inflationary pricing fades, input costs rise, and demand softens. The Fed cut rates during 2024, but the yields on longer-dated treasuries actually rose as the year ended. Stubbornly high borrowing costs continue to plague rate-sensitive areas of the economy, like housing. Employment and inflation data may be volatile in 2025 and could affect changes in monetary policy and lead to greater volatility across equity markets.

Despite resilient economic data and limited signs of credit risk, we believe vigilance is warranted. Our cautious posture persists due to high valuations, market concentration, looming debt challenges, and fraying consumer health. We anticipate lower expected returns in the near term, based on slowing growth (function of restrictive monetary policy) and high valuations. Valuation multiple expansion can only take the market so far (particularly late in a market cycle). We expect a reversion to the mean whereby earnings growth & dividends drive returns going forward. While optimism remains high, the vulnerabilities of momentum-driven leadership highlight the need for discipline. Markets may reward risk-taking in the short term, but lasting wealth is built through patience, real income, and fundamentals.

data.title: VanEck Mid-February 2025 Bitcoin ChainCheck
data.description:
Bitcoin policy momentum builds as Washington reverses course. States push for BTC reserves, and miners accelerate AI/HPC expansion. We break down key market, on-chain, and macro trends.

Please note that VanEck has exposure to bitcoin.

Three key takeaways for mid-January – mid-February:

  1. Washington Reverse Course on Crypto: The U.S. government is pivoting toward a more pro-crypto stance, with new legislative efforts, SEC regulatory shifts, and Bitcoin reserve discussions at the White House reinforcing Bitcoin’s legitimacy.
  2. State Bitcoin Treasuries Gain Momentum, 'Frontrunning’ the Fed: State legislators are rapidly proposing strategic Bitcoin reserves, positioning themselves ahead of federal discussions on a Strategic Bitcoin Reserve (SBR). To quantify the potential impact, we published the Bitcoin Reserve Tool, which helps model Bitcoin’s potential role in U.S. monetary policy.
  3. Miners Expand Capacity, Accelerate AI/HPC Pivot: The 13 miners we cover are on track for a 42% CAGR in energized capacity, with increasing commitments to AI/HPC expansion. As AI energy demand surges, large-scale miners are positioning themselves as emerging AI/HPC infrastructure providers.

Chart of the Month

Relative Unrealized Profit (RUP) Indicators Have Cooled In 2025, Suggesting an Accumulation Opportunity

Context: In our November ChainCheck, we analyzed the relationship between Bitcoin’s Relative Unrealized Profits (RUP) and forward-looking returns. RUP measures the proportion of Bitcoin’s market cap represented by unrealized gains - profits that exist on paper but have not yet been realized through selling.

Insight: Historically, Bitcoin’s strongest short- to medium-term returns (7-day to 180-day) have occurred when RUP’s 30-day moving average (30 DMA) ranged between 0.60 and 0.70—high but not yet overheated. Today, RUP sits at ~0.55, having never exceeded 0.62 since November. Given the short-lived duration of this indicator so far this cycle, we believe today’s reading suggests another accumulation opportunity before a potential market cycle top.

Bitcoin’s Price Action

Market sentiment: Bitcoin is once again in a period of low volatility, with price trading sideways (-2 %) month-over-month. Despite Bitcoin’s price trading sideways, Bitcoin dominance is up 6% month-over-month as altcoins continue to bleed, firmly negating the budding alt season we highlighted in December. This disconnect between Bitcoin and altcoins has led to a dismal market sentiment; while the bullish U.S. regulatory backdrop has allowed Bitcoin to sustain its prices near all-time highs, altcoins have suffered from continued dispersion, most notably as meme coin mania continues. The most recent escalation of this “memecoin” carnage was through the fake Argentine cryptocurrency LIBRA, which was falsely touted by President Javier Milei’s social media.

While damaging, we believe this sort of rampant market behavior invariably highlights Bitcoin’s distinguished position among cryptocurrencies.

Like our call in September, we believe this period of low volatility will end with a Bitcoin breakout to the upside. We also expect altcoin season to resume, reversing Bitcoin’s recent dominance uptrend. However, this will be a token-picker’s market. With far more altcoins available in 2025 than in prior cycles (2016–2017, 2020–2021), we do not expect a rising tide to lift all boats equally.

In anticipation of an upside catalyst, we examine the latest policy developments.

Policy Updates: President Trump appears determined to fulfill his campaign promise to make the U.S. “the Crypto Capital of the World,” taking aggressive steps to reverse the federal government’s prior anti-crypto stance. This is playing out across several legislative, judicial, and executive actions. The House Committee on Financial Services recently held a hearing on "Operation Choke Point 2.0: The Biden Administration’s Efforts to Put Crypto in the Crosshairs," investigating allegations that crypto businesses and individuals were systematically debanked starting in 2021. If confirmed, these actions may have contributed to the collapse of crypto-friendly banks such as Signature Bank and Silvergate.

In late January, SEC Acting Chairman Mark Uyeda announced the formation of a Crypto Task Force to establish a clear regulatory framework for digital assets. Led by SEC Commissioner Hester Peirce, the Task Force signals a shift away from enforcement-first regulation. Its key objectives include defining the security status of digital assets, establishing temporary relief for token issuers, and addressing custody, clearing, and cross-border crypto regulation. As part of this shift, over 50 SEC lawyers and staff have reportedly been reassigned from crypto enforcement actions.

‘Stargate’, a $500 billion AI infrastructure initiative, was also announced in late January. Stargate is led by Softbank’s Chairman Masayoshi Son—who previously pledged $100 billion in AI investment—and has doubled down under Trump’s deregulatory, pro-energy, pro-crypto, pro-AI administration. SoftBank is joined by OpenAI, Oracle, and MGX in the initiative. While not directly tied to digital assets, our research suggests Stargate could provide Bitcoin miners with capital to expand into AI and HPC. Given Bitcoin miners' expertise in securing large-scale power agreements, they could emerge as key players in this AI-driven energy expansion.

SoftBank seems to think so. Later that month, it invested $50 million in Cipher Mining (CIFR), acquiring 10.4 million shares to support CIFR’s HPC data center expansion. This move reinforces the growing recognition of Bitcoin miners as emerging AI/HPC infrastructure providers.

On February 4, White House ‘Crypto Czar’ David Sacks held a joint press conference with congressional leaders, outlining a bipartisan roadmap for digital asset legislation. Congress is advancing several key bills:

  1. Stablecoins: The GENIUS Act (Guiding and Establishing National Innovation in U.S. Stablecoins Act), introduced by Senator Hagerty, defines stablecoins as USD-pegged digital assets, establishes issuance procedures, licensing, and reserve requirements, and designates the Federal Reserve and OCC as primary regulators. Meanwhile, Rep. French Hill's draft Stablecoin Tethering and Bank Licensing Enforcement (STABLE) Act is even more bullish. It permits state-qualified issuers without imposing a market cap limit, allowing for broader competition in the U.S. stablecoin market.
  2. Market Structure: Lawmakers plan to reintroduce FIT21, which would clarify the CFTC’s jurisdiction over digital commodities and grant the SEC oversight of blockchain-based securities.

During the press conference, Sacks stated, “[A] Bitcoin reserve will be one of the first things we are going to look at.”

As the federal government pivots toward a more crypto-friendly stance, individual U.S. states are also accelerating their own Bitcoin strategies. A growing number are moving ahead of Washington by introducing legislation to treat Bitcoin as a Strategic Reserve Asset—a trend we explore next.

This month, we published a summary table of our database tracking the U.S. States’ legislative initiatives to invest in Bitcoin and other digital assets. After reading each piece of legislation, we identified the sources of funding and estimated their sizes by looking up their most recently available financials. Based on the legislation’s limitations (e.g., up to 10% of the General Fund), we estimated the potential investment size in U.S. dollars, which we then converted to Bitcoin. For simplicity, we excluded other cryptocurrencies, though several bills theoretically allow for investment in other digital assets contingent on certain factors such as market cap.

At current market prices of ~$95,350 per Bitcoin, we estimated that currently the pending legislative initiatives could see these U.S. states purchase a total for 262,577 BTC, worth ~$25 billion. This figure is both optimistic and conservative. The probability of each of these proposals passing is low, as three bills from Pennsylvania, Wyoming, and North Dakota have already failed. Further, our estimates are based on the upper bounds of each bill, which means purchasing as much Bitcoin as each bill allows. On the other hand, however, several bills do not provide clear guidance to estimate the amount of Bitcoin they could acquire. Thus, we allocated zero Bitcoin purchases to them. We also emphasize that while some of these bills include allocations to Bitcoin in certain state retirement funds, this list excludes pension-only allocations already made in states like Michigan and Wisconsin.

Regardless of the near-term likelihood of these bills passing and making significant allocations towards the digital asset class, this exercise illustrates the shift in sentiment between U.S. public policy and digital assets. These developments were previously unthinkable under prior administrations, given their hostility towards crypto entrepreneurs, banks, etc., as demonstrated by the systemic debanking now being uncovered in the Operation Chokepoint 2.0 investigations. As of February 17th, 32 pieces of state Bitcoin & digital asset legislation have been submitted—24 of them strategic reserves—suggesting the urgency of this matter perceived by state lawmakers.

At The Federal Level: A U.S. Strategic Bitcoin Reserve Could Help Offset National Debt

As states move ahead with Bitcoin reserves, they are effectively ‘frontrunning’ the federal government's emerging policy signals for a Strategic Bitcoin Reserve (SBR). While no formal U.S. Treasury initiative exists yet, the debate is gaining momentum.

To explore this further, we’ve developed the U.S. Debt to Bitcoin Reserve Tool, which is now available on our website. This interactive model lets users adjust U.S. debt growth rates, Bitcoin acquisition prices, BTC price growth, and reserve size to visualize how a national Bitcoin reserve could impact U.S. debt.

With state adoption accelerating, this tool offers a data-driven framework for evaluating Bitcoin’s potential role in U.S. monetary policy. This idea, once speculative, is now increasingly entering the policy debate.

Bitcoin Miners

Publicly Traded Bitcoin Miners Will Energize an Estimated 4+ GW Per Year Through 2027

Scaling Electrical Capacity

We conducted a comprehensive mapping of the 13 public Bitcoin miners in our coverage: (BITF), (BTBT), (BTDR), (CIFR), (CLSK), (CORZ), (GLXY), (HIVE), (HUT), (IREN), (MARA), (RIOT), and (WULF). We estimate that these miners collectively operate 7.1GW of energized capacity, with plans to expand to 11.7GW by the end of 2025, 15.9GW by 2026, and 20.4GW by 2027. This projection represents a 42% compound annual growth rate over three years. Beyond 2028, they have an additional 7.3GW pipeline, which we view as a conservative estimate, given the competitive nature of power procurement in Bitcoin mining and the more secretive nature of long-term growth pipelines.

Assuming the expansion through 2027 would require a modernized (15 J/TH efficiency) fleet of Bitmain Antminer S21 Pros at $5,000 each and $450,000 per MW of supporting infrastructure, we estimate the total cost of this growth would be $24.8 billion in capex.

However, we think miners are unlikely to commit all this capacity to Bitcoin. Securing new electrical capacity is a core competency for miners—one that is becoming even more valuable in the era of power-intensive AI computing. According to Goldman Sachs, AI currently consumes ~7.7GW of global data center power usage (14%), a figure expected to grow to ~22.7GW (27%) by 2027. Similarly, based on our review of their site pipelines, we think the Bitcoin miners we cover will pivot at least 20%-30% of their electrical capacity to supporting AI/HPC workloads.

Miners' AI/HPC Pivot Accelerates

The shift toward AI and high-performance computing (HPC) is gaining momentum among Bitcoin miners. Following CoreWeave’s 700MW AI/HPC deal in 2024, multiple miners are now actively exploring AI-driven revenue streams:

  • Bitfarms (BITF): Engaged two AI/HPC consultants to assess feasibility across all North American sites.
  • Bitdeer (BTDR): Following completion of its AI/HPC datacenter consulting engagement, cited ongoing discussions with AI/HPC development partners.
  • Cipher Mining (CIFR): In late January, SoftBank invested $50 million in Cipher Mining, acquiring 10.4 million shares to support HPC data center expansion.
  • Riot (RIOT): Announced additions to its Board of Directors bringing AI/HPC conversion, data center, and real estate expertise, in addition to retaining AI/HPC investment banking expertise.
  • HIVE Digital Technologies (HIVE): Appointed Craig Tavares as President and COO of Buzz HPC to lead the company’s growth in HPC and GPU cloud services.
  • Iris Energy (IREN): Announced a 75MW liquid-cooled AI/HPC data center in Childress, TX (H2 2025) and an additional 600MW expansion at Sweetwater, bringing total site capacity to 2GW—potentially making it one of the largest sites in North America that may become AI/HPC-eligible.

One industry expert explained the scarcity of such large-scale sites like IREN’s, stating, "I can count on two hands the number of people who have 1,000MW [at one site] today." Such campuses are ideal for AI training, providing the dense compute clusters needed for cutting-edge model development, faster iteration, and sustained workloads at scale. This underscores the strategic advantage of miners with large portfolios of scalable energy assets as AI demand surges.

Implications for Bitcoin Miners

Bitcoin Network Transaction Fees Have Failed to Keep Pace with Price

The pivot toward AI/HPC is increasingly critical as Bitcoin’s fee revenue model remains unreliable. While network congestion associated with speculative markets can drive temporary spikes in transaction fees, sustained on-chain revenue growth remains elusive. Meanwhile, the rise of off-chain solutions (ETFs, futures markets, L2s, centralized exchanges) could further reduce miners' long-term fee income.

With Bitcoin’s block rewards halving every four years, BTC’s price must double just for miners to maintain their revenues. This structural challenge further highlights why AI/HPC is an attractive diversification play.

We believe the most successful Bitcoin miners will be those that:

  1. Use mining to subsidize grid expansion in remote and overlooked energy markets.
  2. Diversify into AI/HPC by leveraging their existing power portfolios and pipelines for higher-margin services, unlocking new financing vehicles.
  3. Monetize operational expertise through innovations such as liquid cooling systems, chip design, and mining co-location technologies.

Bitcoin’s Network Activity, Adoption, and Fees

Daily Transactions: Transaction activity declined modestly, down 10% MoM.

Ordinals Inscriptions: Ordinals transactions are down 6% month-over-month (MoM), losing momentum after the Q4 surge in activity. Like NFTs on other blockchains, we view Ordinals as on-chain collectibles—essentially luxury or Veblen goods—whose demand tends to rise and fall with Bitcoin’s price performance. This cyclical pattern suggests that while inscriptions may cool during periods of consolidation, they could reaccelerate in the next bull phase, particularly with continued advances in marketplace and gallery user experiences.

Total Transfer Volume: Transfer volume is down 28% MoM; we believe low volatility is once again the culprit. One month ago, transfer volumes were high as Bitcoin prices bounced off a low of $89k on January 13th and rapidly approached new highs above $109k by January 20th.

Average Transaction Fees: Costing an average of $1.40, Bitcoin fees are down 23% MoM, reflecting the reduced demand from transactions, transfer volume, and ordinals activity.

Global Power Consumption & Mining Difficulty: Global power consumption grew 3% to reach 157 TWh, and mining difficulty rose 8% MoM to 114 terahashes (T), both hitting all-time highs. This reflects a robust and growing network as miners scale operations to meet rising Bitcoin demand.

Total Crypto Equities Market Cap: Down 1% MoM, crypto equities mirrored Bitcoin’s low volatility and minimal price changes MoM.

Transfer Volume from Miners to Exchanges: Miners are transferring ~$7.4M per day to exchanges, up 35% MoM. These levels reflect the 94th percentile of all-time history, though they are still less than half of the levels seen throughout most of H1’21. We think that transfer volumes from miners to exchanges are a good proxy for Bitcoin’s adoption as a monetary asset, as higher levels reflect a market capable of absorbing new, higher-cost supply as mining difficulty increases.

Futures Sentiment: Annualized Bitcoin futures declined 30% MoM, reflecting a shift from last month’s bullish sentiment to a more neutral. At the 51st percentile of historical levels, funding rates suggest market indecision rather than outright bearishness.

data.title: 2 Glorious Dividends To Retire In Style, Yields +7.5%
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities

Many of us are careful with our money, diligently tracking expenses and optimizing our spending, even when there's no financial pressure to do so. Yet, despite our best efforts, maintaining the same lifestyle year after year becomes increasingly expensive. This is the harsh reality of inflation economics - a silent, immoral tax that penalizes the diligent and the cautious more than anyone else.

For those still in the workforce, annual salary raises often serve as a buffer against inflation, helping to preserve purchasing power and lifestyle. Similarly, the interest earned on savings accounts might barely keep pace with inflation, but let's be honest: no one ever got rich by keeping their money in a savings account.

So, how do retirees protect their lifestyle from the erosive effects of inflation? There are many possible approaches that retirees could pursue, but my approach is to let the financial markets pay me a growing amount of money every year to combat the effects of inflation. The global economy is a massive, dynamic machine, with trillions of dollars exchanging hands daily, goods and services are being bought and sold in enormous volumes. Dividends are a way to tap into that enormous amount of capital and direct a portion into your account. 2024 was a record year for dividend payouts, and the global aggregate dividends are projected to stay at $2.3 trillion in 2025, with North America and Asia Pacific delivering strong growth.

By investing in companies that pay substantial dividends and consistently raise them and by strategically reinvesting a portion of your dividend income, you can protect your lifestyle in retirement. Let us now discuss two picks to put this strategy into action.

Pick #1: BTI - Yield 7.7%

Thanks to social stigma and the fear of regulatory blockades on their key products, big tobacco companies have been severely undervalued in recent years. There is a tremendous benefit to buying undervalued companies that pay large dividends; you get paid handsomely to wait for the sentiment to change, and it will be beneficial even if that change is small.

British American Tobacco (BTI), which will be referred to as BAT in this article, is the largest tobacco company, with operations in over 180 markets globally. BAT has significantly outperformed the S&P 500 and the Consumer Staples index, demonstrating the power of resilient cash flows from an indispensable industry.

Why do I say indispensable about a product that is known to be harmful to human health?

  • Widespread use: In 2021, an estimated 46 million U.S. adults reported currently using any tobacco product, including cigarettes, e-cigarettes, cigars, smokeless tobacco, and pipes (including hookah). Among those who used tobacco products, 77.5% reported using combustible products (cigarettes, cigars, or pipes), and 18.1% reported using two or more tobacco products. Smoking has an allure of being cool, popularized by Hollywood. Despite the negative perception towards combustible cigarettes, e-cigarettes and vapes have taken over as the new symbol of modern smoking culture. Marketed as "less harmful" alternatives, they come with sleek designs, customizable flavors, and a tech-driven appeal. They are particularly popular among younger demographics, partly due to social media, influencer culture, and targeted marketing.

  • Deeply ingrained into society (and the political system): Smoking is extremely prevalent in society, and proposals to ban it have political ramifications. Although flavored cigarettes have been banned in the U.S. since 2009, menthol is the lone exception. Menthol cigarettes comprise a third of the industry's total U.S. market share. The Biden administration expressed an interest in banning menthol cigarettes in 2021 and put together a formal proposal, only to postpone the decision fearing a negative reaction from voters repeatedly. The Trump administration has withdrawn this plan altogether. Former U.K. Prime Minister Rishi Sunak proposed an age-tiered smoking ban, which would gradually phase out cigarette sales by preventing younger generations from ever legally purchasing them. However, this bill failed to get the support of his own caucus.

  • Tax Revenues: Tobacco is the most highly taxed consumer product in the United States. In 2023, the U.S. brought in over $37 billion in revenue from taxing cigarettes and other tobacco products at the federal, state, and local government levels. Despite higher taxation (seen as a punitive measure) and consistent price increases, demand for tobacco products remains strong, highlighting its deeply ingrained role in society and the challenges of reducing consumption.

We are not here to debate the morality of investing in tobacco, as moral arguments rarely justify an investment decision in publicly traded companies. Social media's addictive impact on mental health, labor exploitation in countries with weak labor laws, and the dumping of toxic waste into oceans are just a few ethical dilemmas that investors must navigate. Smoking is yet another issue, and ultimately, investors must decide what aligns with their values - and what lets them sleep at night.

Last year, BAT sold a 3.6% stake in India-based ITC Ltd. for £1.57 billion, using the proceeds to repurchase shares. The company continues to maintain a 25% stake in the Indian conglomerate, which went through a demerger last year. As a result, BAT became a 15.3% shareholder in ITC hotels (whose shares were issued to ITC shareholders as part of the transaction). This 15.3% stake is worth $630 million, and BAT's CEO mentioned that the company doesn't see this to be a strategic fit in their portfolio and plans to divest by 2026.

"We have no interest in becoming a long-term shareholder of a hotel chain in India," said Tadeu Marroco, Chief Executive Officer of BAT.

During FY 2024, BAT added 3.6 million adult consumers to a total of 29.1 million to its smokeless products, which represent 18% of FY 2024 revenues. BAT's Vuse maintains an impressive 64.8% market share in Europe, with the percentage exceeding 85% in key markets like France, Poland, Denmark, and Switzerland. Within the European Union, the smokeless business contributes over 30% of the revenues in 18 markets.

BAT is a highly cash-flow-rich business, generating £7.9 billion in FCF during FY 2024, and the company maintains a strong track record of sharing profits with shareholders. BAT maintains a £900 million share buyback plan for 2025 (after £700 million was used in FY 2024). BAT has returned over £30 billion to shareholders since 2018.

With its recent 2% annual dividend raise for FY 2024, BAT boasts 18 years of growing dividend payments, and its current GBp 60.06/share quarterly payment calculates to a 7.7% annualized yield.

Note: BAT is a U.K. corporation that declares and pays dividends in GBP. U.S. investors will receive an amount that is variable based on exchange rates. The U.K. does not withhold taxes on dividends paid to international investors.

From a valuation standpoint, BTI offers a high yield with the highest coverage in its peer group while trading at the lowest multiple. At current prices, we also like Altria (MO), but we will discuss this Dividend King in a separate article.

BAT has warned of headwinds to its combustibles business from increased taxation of tobacco among other products in Bangladesh due to the IMF's special ordinance, and increased regulatory pressures in Australia. Big Tobacco's current valuations are reflective of these headwinds, and BAT is currently leading in the adoption of smokeless products. The company targets becoming a fully smokeless company by 2035, and the company has a strong platform of products that support this vision and are seeing growing adoption in major markets. By 2030, the company aims to have 50 million consumers of its smokeless products.

BAT has had a terrific year, and we expect great things from this company in 2025 and beyond. In an overvalued market, there is a significant benefit to buying discounted companies. When the biggest growth story runs out of steam, reliable cash-flow giants like BAT keep delivering, reminding investors that fundamentals always matter.

Pick #2: CSWC - Yield 10.1%

Capital Southwest Corporation (CSWC) is an internally managed BDC (Business Development Company) that invests in first lien senior secured loans to lower middle market companies with annual EBITDA between $3M and $25M. CSWC is giving us a little extra income this quarter, with regular dividends of $0.58/share and a supplemental dividend of $0.06/share. The supplement dividend last quarter was $0.05, and the BDC has paid a supplemental dividend for 10 quarters in a row.

CSWC has a great track record of producing excess earnings and paying supplements and special dividends. However, investors must note that the supplement can disappear without any fundamental issue with the company. Source

During the third quarter (CSWC's fiscal year ends on March 31st), the BDC reported pre-tax NII of $0.64/share, and NAV remained unchanged at $16.59/share. 95% of CSWC's portfolio was rated 1 or 2, indicating that the borrower is performing at or better than at the time of loan origination.

CSWC pays a 10.1% annualized yield when factoring in the annualized base yield and only the declared supplemental dividends.

Conclusion

Inflation is an unavoidable reality, silently eroding the purchasing power of your savings and threatening your lifestyle and financial security in retirement.

By investing in companies that distribute a portion of their profits to shareholders-and strategically reinvesting a portion of those dividends-you can create a self-sustaining income stream that grows to combat inflation. This approach not only safeguards your purchasing power but also positions you to benefit from the dynamic global markets.

At High Dividend Opportunities, we make this happen through our Rule of 42, which emphasizes deep diversification for reliable income, and the rule of 25, which insists on reinvesting at least 25% of the total income generated back into the portfolio for organic growth, inflation defense, and to take advantage of market volatility. The key to a secure retirement isn't just preserving your wealth-it's ensuring that your income keeps growing, year after year. This is the power of the Income Method, and the beauty of income investing.

data.title: Michael Lee On The AI Revolution - Palantir Still The Clear Leader
data.description:
Originally published on February 20, 2025

By Mike Larson

In this episode of the MoneyShow MoneyMasters Podcast, Michael Lee, founder of Michael Lee Strategies, shares why he believes Palantir Technologies is the clear AI leader and why Wall Street is still underestimating the AI revolution.

Transcript

Larson: At our MoneyShow Symposium in December 2024, my next guest dedicated his entire presentation to one tech stock. No, really, in front of the audience in Sarasota, he called it the clear leader ready to take over the future. And between that date and today, the stock is already up 54%.

What does the 20-plus-year veteran of the financial services industry think about the name now? And what else does he like in the tech sector or otherwise? Let's find out. My guest is Michael Lee, founder of the investment and wealth planning firm, Michael Lee Strategies. Michael, welcome to the MoneyShow MoneyMasters Podcast.

So, Mike, it's not very often that a guest talks about one stock entirely from the main stage, but that's what you did in Sarasota back in December. So what is that stock and why do you do it?

Lee: So that stock is Palantir (PLTR). The stock is up on unconscionable amounts over the last year. I think from the low-20s to almost 120, as we're speaking right now. And it's – it was expensive on a price-to-earnings ratio and a price-to-sales ratio at 20, it's much more expensive now. And the question is why? The world has changed from a data management standpoint. In that, the way data is ingested to a corporation and used on a day-to-day basis is forever changed. We're never going back.

And I'd liken this change to moving from a horse and buggy to an internal combustion engine, or from hand-making things to an all completely automated assembly line. Might even be a better comparison. And that, of course, is artificial intelligence. And so we're, I think, two-and-a-half weeks off of DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK), which rocked the markets. And that touched on a lot of topics and the market, read it the opposite way it should have, in that something called Jevons paradox in that where the faster and more advanced a technology gets and the cheaper it gets, the more widely used it is.

And so if I can get 10x or 20x to compute or computing power out of Nvidia (NVDA) GPU now, as before DeepSeek, it makes much more sense to invest in one because my return on investment is exponentially higher. So we're at a turning point, I think, Wall Street is completely missing it.

But to bring it back to Palantir, what's going on with these data is these data – it all goes into a large language model. And for that large language model to be applied to a business, you need a software solution, and Palantir is without competition.

And so as people are starting to get their arms around about how big this artificial intelligence, this data revolution that's going on right now is, they're starting to see that there's a clear leader in the space, and they're $4 billion in revenue next year, may actually be $5 billion and '28, '29, maybe $20 billion or $25 billion. That's $10-plus billion in free cash flow, $15 billion in free cash flow, which could make it $1 trillion company, given the margins and how spectacular their technology is.

And it's - they've been doing this for so long, was funded by the CIA in 2004. And so now we're 20 years into them, starting figuring out these problems, solving these problems, and they've only had an enterprise or corporate product for two years.

So like all these forces are converging, I would love to be able to go back and buy it like in the low-70s. And we may well get a roundtrip back there. But I've never seen a turning point in my career in the way that we're seeing things right now.

Larson: Fair enough. Mike, I want to ask you about the use cases. I kind of think that was one of the most fascinating parts of the story there, that back in December when you talked about Palantir is the different industries that are using their tech, and how is that going to evolve?

Lee: So Palantir's – their CTO goes on CNBC and they - talks about this on their YouTube channel in that, like if you're buying Palantir software, you're looking for ROI day one, not a project that works two and three years down the road where you have like, you buy Salesforce (CRM) and you got to spend more on consultants implementing it than you do on the software. And they're still, look, how well Salesforce has done. Palantir, they want to come in and work on things right away.

So one of the best examples and way to conceptually visualize this is what they've done in hospitals. So if you walk into an ER, there's probably a nurse or a doctor or an administrator, but a couple of people sitting at the front desk taking your data input. And then they're manually sitting there saying, hey, this person has a broken arm, we're going to send him to this room and assign this doctor. That person needs stitches, they'll go over there. This person is having a heart attack, they'll go over there. Well, what happens with – but it's a manual process.

And to try to solve these problems, like you have smart people that have their logic about how they want to allocate doctors to rooms and staff. But their goal for patient care is to get people seen as quickly as possible from a business standpoint, for the hospitals to free up as much emergency room space as possible and get that turnover almost akin to a restaurant.

So at the end of 2022, they had zero hospitals as clients. Now they – now it's close to a quarter of the hospital beds in the country are being run by Palantir. And so what they have done is they've automated that process of allocating patients to rooms, assigning doctors to the rooms, and using machine learning to predict staffing for the hospitals.

And so at Tampa General, right by us, they've been able to do that with 95% accuracy to use machine learning to say, hey, we need this many nurses on staff, this many doctors on staff, okay?

Another unbelievable use case is AIG, just talked about it on their earnings call. In that, Palantir is essential to their underwriting process. And so when they go into underwrite for business, they can produce quotes in three hours in a process that used to take two weeks. And so if you think about what happens there at an underwriting process, you're looking at all the different forms of risk, all the pricing to reinsure it, all the past historical claims that they've had, your predictive models to the future. Historically, there's been a team of smart people sitting there, putting all these pieces together manually and then figuring it out. Hey, that just happens instantaneously right now. Those same smart people are working on the inputs, but the machine is in a sense making the sausage in a couple of hours, what everybody could do in a few weeks.

You can now bid on infinitely more business than you could prior. Time will tell whether it's more predictive, but it's a distinct competitive advantage where there is like almost a day one return of investment, profitable investment for someone going in and buying the software. And how it's gone historically in all these tech trends is first you have the hardware, you build up the infrastructure, then you have the software to implement it.

Larson: Got it. As much as I'd love to talk about Palantir for our entire segment, I think, I'd be cheating our listeners, our viewers, if we didn't look at some of the other ideas that you have out there. I mean, as you survey this market, as we're chatting, NASDAQ has been a little choppy so far this year after a great '24. What other kind of names have you heard about in this earnings cycle that you think have a good story too?

Lee: So again, like I just don't think the market has their arms around how big this AI trend is, and you're looking at trillions of dollars of investment. And if you think about data on a whole as a company takes it in, whatever the organization does, whether it's a nonprofit, whether it's a government organization, whether it's a for-profit company, either a private or public, every bit of data is going to get fed into a large language model at some point in the future.

We do not have anywhere near the amount of infrastructure to figure that out. And so if you look at Microsoft (MSFT) in their earnings, the stock got crushed because they missed on data center growth by, I think, 1.1 percentage point. And Google (GOOG), Amazon (AMZN), and Microsoft are all saying the same thing in that they are missing because they are capacity-constrained, meaning the demand far outstrips the supply.

And so, Nvidia, for every GPU they sell, they have demand for 15. And so the Street is looking at all of these names in this complex, like what's the ROI? Like how much earnings am I going to see next quarter without realizing again, like we've just gone from having 50 people in a room, assembling parts by hand to an automated assembly line.

So every organization is going to need to have this as a part of their data intake. And if you look at the way data is being created, so in 2025, we're going to generate as much data as we were predicted to in 2030. And so like the Internet of Things and everything, it's all like a hockey stick growth.

So as more data comes in, as it gets cheaper to compute, the more projects are going to get built out. And like, I don't like to use the word AI or Gen AI because I think of Terminator 2 and like the T-1000 running at you where it's really just – it's just taking data. It's taking that underwriting process that was two weeks long and getting it down to three hours, soon enough an hour, soon enough 30 minutes. Like that's what we're thinking or every single decision, anybody makes of significance is somewhat data-based, and it's organizing all that data and the amount of data that's being created.

So at AMD (AMD), again, like they beat sales, beat estimates, raise guidance, wasn't quite as much as the Street wanted. The stock got crushed. It's trading at 15x forward earnings. And in reality, it's probably more like 12x forward earnings because the earnings will beat. So to me, AMD is just a screaming buy. Their Chief Commercial Officer just bought $1 million worth of stock.

So like you could say whatever you want about insider selling. But what an insider reaches into his own pocket to buy the stock, especially with the stock awards all these executives get in the Q, it's really an unbelievable opportunity.

And so like, obviously, Nvidia is 135, it was at 150, roughly six months ago, and their sales have been – the sales and revenue have increased substantially. The numbers are too low going out a year. So you're looking at '24, '25, four times forward earnings for them, where it's historically traded between 35 and 40.

And then you can go down this infrastructure play, Google, Microsoft, Amazon, just – Oracle (ORCL), all of this, the runway is just so much bigger than the Street’s gives credit for. And again, like this is the one-time in my career where I can just say the majority of sell-side analysts and a majority of buy-side investors, they cannot see the forest from the trees with kind of the single greatest opportunity that's out there.

So I just – seeing this in front of me, the biggest companies, I'm absolutely as bullish as can be. This has nothing to do with tariffs. This has nothing to do with inflation. This has nothing to do with interest rates. This is a trend that's happening, whether you like it or not. And the Street is missing the size of it, which is why like a Palantir can get pushed by mostly retail investors from the low-20s to almost 120 inside of a year because it takes a little bit more vision, a little foresight into the future.

Larson: Got it. You touched on some of those macro points. I think we could probably have that for another discussion. There's a lot to cover there, but I do want to briefly just ask you, given where we are, sort of in the economic cycle, political cycle, whatever, anything outside of tech, sector-wise or name-wise that you think looks pretty attractive here, too?

Lee: So going on that insider buying trend, Ally Financial (ALLY), a lot of the executives went in and bought and like there's a lot of noise around used car defaults and Ally Financial is the old GMAC, doing auto loans, trading a 1x book, 3% dividend yield. It doesn't seem super crazy or sexy. But the reality is like you probably got – you're going to have book value increase over the next year, and it could go to 1.3x or 1.4x book within the next year. It's a 30% or 40% in the sleepy stock. That's where I see some upside there.

And so Tesla (TSLA) is a car company, but it's – so – automated autonomous driving. So Tesla thinks they're going to have their – they're going to have driverless taxis in Austin by the summer, which is mind-boggling. That’s one that – that'll be one of the first things they've had early because that was a 2026 thing. And then they have this robotics division on the other end, and the stock’s at 340, 350 down from over 400, it's worth a look at there. And it's a call option on, okay, do you want a robot in your home to do your laundry, wash your floor, do your dishes, and all sorts of cool stuff thinking two or three years from now?

And then the other one is Mobileye (MBLY). So Mobileye is an autonomous driving software. And so, there's other people out there that have the chips, but Mobileye already has a deal with VW to be the autonomous driving partner for Volkswagen (OTCPK:VWAGY). In 2026, it's going to be in like a third of their cars, but autonomous driving is happening.

And the way that I see it playing out is that people with longer commutes that are driving 20 to 50 minutes on the highway or an hour and a half on the highway to and from work, when you get to level three autonomous driving, you don't need to have your hands on the wheel. You don't need to pay attention. But you can be looking at your phone where if you're in a Tesla right now, and you have the autonomous driving, you don't need your hands on the wheel.

But if you pick up your phone, like you get an alert, like it's not, like the level three, like for those long highway miles, so someone like you going from coast-to-coast, when you're on Alligator Alley, you could be working, instead of paying attention to the road and then from there, I think people get more comfortable with it, taking them off the highway and to their home. So the question for them becomes, do GM and Ford and Honda and Hyundai, okay, do they contract out to Waymo? Do they use Tesla software? Or do they use Mobileye?

Mobileye is your only pure play in the area. The stock has just been crushed. They're already in a lot of cars. If you have a newer car, and you're getting close to somebody and all of a sudden, your car starts automatically breaking and beeping, okay, chances are that's a Mobileye software. So they already have the level three autonomous software that works. And it's just a matter of how many more partners beyond VW can get. And this is an essential component for automakers because this autonomous driving feature will be a monthly payment. You're going to pay $30 or $50. And the first time you saw this was BMW tried to charge for heated seats. It didn't go so well, but there's no reoccurring revenue for these OEMs, for these automakers.

So to be able to charge a subscription fee on a monthly basis to the people that buy their cars, I mean, that's a huge upside. Mobileye will not only get paid to get in the cars, they'll get a percentage of that revenue. So like this could be a 10x in the next four or five years. There's a chance Qualcomm (QCOM) who makes the chips, but doesn't have a software solution, takes them out far above where they are right now. It's a relatively cheap stock.
